item.title: Minnesota governor mobilizes National Guard to battle wildfires in northern part of state
item.description:
July 12, 20268:32 PM EDTUpdated 1 hour ago

Minnesota Governor Tim Walz looks on during a House Oversight Committee hearing with U.S. governors about state policies regarding undocumented migrants, on Capitol Hill in Washington, D.C., U.S., June 12, 2025. REUTERS/Kevin Lamarque Purchase Licensing Rights
item.content:encoded:
July 12, 20268:32 PM EDTUpdated 1 hour ago

Minnesota Governor Tim Walz looks on during a House Oversight Committee hearing with U.S. governors about state policies regarding undocumented migrants, on Capitol Hill in Washington, D.C., U.S., June 12, 2025. REUTERS/Kevin Lamarque Purchase Licensing Rights

July 12 - Minnesota Governor Tim Walz mobilized state National Guard troops on Sunday ‚Äãto battle wildfires on the state's ‚Äånorthern border with Canada.

The fires have covered more than 800 acres in five days and are yet ‚Äãto be contained, according to a ‚ÄãMinnesota state website. They are among the ‚ÅÝlatest in a swath of fires ‚Äãthat have raged across the United States due ‚Äãto hot, dry conditions driven by an El Nino weather pattern across the continent.

Stay up to date on the key companies, data, and decisions in the ESG world with the Reuters Sustainable Finance newsletter. Sign up here.

"I‚Äôve declared a peacetime ‚Äãemergency and mobilized the National Guard to ‚Äãassist with wildfire response in northern Minnesota," said Walz, ‚Äåa ‚ÅÝDemocrat. "Minnesotans extend our deepest gratitude to all of the first responders working around the clock to keep their neighbors safe."

A total ‚Äãof 16 fires ‚Äãare ‚ÅÝburning in the Superior National Forest in northern Minnesota, which normally ‚Äãattracts about 150,000 visitors in the ‚Äãsummer. ‚ÅÝPark rangers have been escorting visitors out of the park over the weekend, according to ‚ÅÝthe ‚ÄãMinnesota Natural Resources Department website.

High ‚Äãtemperatures over the weekend increased the fire risk, according ‚Äãto the National Weather Service.

item.title: DOJ investigating allegations around UAW President Shawn Fain
item.description:
July 12, 20266:32 PM EDTUpdated 1 hour ago

United Auto Workers (UAW) President Shawn Fain delivers the keynote address at the UAW 2026 CAP Conference, the national conference of the union's political arm attended by top union officials, in Washington, D.C., U.S., February 9, 2026. REUTERS/Evelyn Hockstein/File Photo/File Photo Purchase Licensing Rights
item.content:encoded:
July 12, 20266:32 PM EDTUpdated 1 hour ago

United Auto Workers (UAW) President Shawn Fain delivers the keynote address at the UAW 2026 CAP Conference, the national conference of the union's political arm attended by top union officials, in Washington, D.C., U.S., February 9, 2026. REUTERS/Evelyn Hockstein/File Photo/File Photo Purchase Licensing Rights

A June 18 monitor email said DOJ initiated a grand jury investigation

Probe examines whether Fain improperly benefited his fianc√©e, union emails show

Fain says allegations are false and tied to monitor's Gaza ceasefire dispute

DETROIT, July 12 (Reuters) - The Department of Justice is probing ‚Äãallegations that United Auto Workers President Shawn Fain took actions to improperly benefit his fianc√©e and retaliated against another ‚Äåsenior union member who objected to the actions, according to internal union documents reviewed by Reuters.

The lead counsel for the federal watchdog appointed to oversee union activities informed Fain and the senior union member, Rich Boyer, last month that the DOJ had initiated a grand jury investigation into matters detailed in the monitor's reports, including ‚Äãthose involving Fain and his fianc√©e. The monitor‚Äôs office, led by New York attorney Neil Barofsky, issues periodic reports about the ‚Äãunion‚Äôs inner workings.

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

Fain, who has denied the monitor's findings and called the allegations outlined by the monitor "bogus," is ‚ÅÝcampaigning for a second four-year term as the leader of the union, a nd an election is scheduled for later this year. Boyer is one ‚Äãof a handful of candidates opposing Fain.

The monitor issued a report last month finding that Fain had retaliated against Boyer and improperly used his authority, ‚Äãincluding in ways that would benefit his fianc√©e, but deferred a decision on disciplinary action pending further review without citing the federal probe.

"We are not publishing the details of our factual findings on this issue at this time out of deference to a Grand Jury investigation DOJ has initiated into that issue," the monitor's lead ‚Äãcounsel wrote in a June 18 email reviewed by Reuters. "We do not intend to publicly disclose the existence of that investigation at this ‚Äãtime," he continued.

Bloomberg earlier reported that the DOJ was probing allegations around Fain. It is unclear how the investigation affects Fain's candidacy.

The UAW declined to comment. ‚ÄåA lawyer ‚ÅÝrepresenting the union said it is not the subject of a grand jury investigation. Boyer and his attorney didn't immediately respond to requests for comment.

In a statement responding to the DOJ investigation, Fain said that Boyer had "fed the monitor false allegations," and that the monitor himself had a "political grudge against me because the UAW took an anti-war stance about what was happening in Gaza." The union president last month said that ‚ÄãBarofsky's reports were politically motivated, citing ‚Äãwhat he said was a ‚ÅÝheated and highly personal disagreement with the monitor in 2024 regarding a call by the union‚Äôs executive board for a ceasefire in Gaza.

"What the Monitor is doing is wrong, it‚Äôs unfair to the UAW and to ‚Äãyou as members, and my lawyers are looking at any and all legal options I can pursue ‚Äãto make it ‚ÅÝstop," Fain said in a statement, adding that he retained a law firm to fight the allegations.

The Department of Justice didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.

The UAW has been under federal oversight since a 2020 settlement to resolve a corruption scandal. The offense involved more than a dozen ‚ÅÝunion officials, ‚Äãsome of whom pleaded guilty to embezzling millions of dollars for their personal benefit, ‚Äãusing the funds to purchase expensive liquor and cigars and to pay for golfing outings and related equipment, and expensive hotel stays. Two former union presidents were sentenced to ‚Äãprison time.

item.title: McConnell says he is unable to return to US Senate yet
item.description:
July 12, 20265:31 PM EDTUpdated 1 hour ago

Item 1 of 3 U.S. Senate Appropriations Subcommittee on Defense Chair Senator Mitch McConnell (R-KY) speaks during a hearing on Capitol Hill in Washington, D.C., U.S, May 19, 2026. REUTERS/Tom Brenner
item.content:encoded:
July 12, 20265:31 PM EDTUpdated 1 hour ago

Item 1 of 3 U.S. Senate Appropriations Subcommittee on Defense Chair Senator Mitch McConnell (R-KY) speaks during a hearing on Capitol Hill in Washington, D.C., U.S, May 19, 2026. REUTERS/Tom Brenner

[1/3]U.S. Senate Appropriations Subcommittee on Defense Chair Senator Mitch McConnell (R-KY) speaks during a hearing on Capitol Hill in Washington, D.C., U.S, May 19, 2026. REUTERS/Tom Brenner Purchase Licensing Rights

July 12 (Reuters) - U.S. Senator Mitch McConnell said he will not ‚Äãrejoin the Senate when it returns to work on ‚ÄåMonday because he is still recovering from a fall and from pneumonia.

"As much as it frustrates me, this process takes time," McConnell said in a ‚Äãstatement on Sunday, his first since he was hospitalized last month. "And ‚Äãon the advice of my doctors, I won‚Äôt be ‚ÅÝable to return to the Senate floor to vote quite yet."

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

He ‚Äãsaid that he suffered a fall in mid-June that left him ‚Äãbriefly unconscious. While hospitalized, he developed pneumonia and was treated with antibiotics, according to a separate statement that McConnell's office attributed to the attending physician. ‚ÄãThe physician was not identified.

McConnell, 84, said he is now at ‚Äãa rehabilitation center, which he did not name.

He will focus on "physical therapy and ‚Äåstrategies ‚ÅÝto reduce his risk of future falls," according to the physician's statement.

The Kentucky Republican and former Senate majority leader, who now chairs the Senate Rules Committee, said he has been working with his ‚Äãlegislative staff on ‚Äãcurrent issues ‚ÅÝand keeping in touch with Senate colleagues.

McConnell has been out of public view since mid-June, when he ‚Äãwas taken from his home to a hospital ‚Äãin the ‚ÅÝWashington area for reasons that were not disclosed until the latest statement.

Less than a day earlier, the office of U.S. Senator Lindsey Graham, a ‚ÅÝSouth ‚ÄãCarolina Republican and one of the ‚Äãchamber's most prominent members, announced that he had died from a heart ailment.

item.title: SoFi Stadium's World Cup run boosts confidence ahead of Super Bowl, LA Olympics
item.description:
July 12, 20266:02 AM EDTUpdated 3 hours ago

Item 1 of 2 Jun 28, 2026; Inglewood, California, USA; A general overall view of Los Angeles Stadium (SoFi Stadium). Mandatory Credit: Kirby Lee-Imagn Images
item.content:encoded:
July 12, 20266:02 AM EDTUpdated 3 hours ago

Item 1 of 2 Jun 28, 2026; Inglewood, California, USA; A general overall view of Los Angeles Stadium (SoFi Stadium). Mandatory Credit: Kirby Lee-Imagn Images

[1/2]Jun 28, 2026; Inglewood, California, USA; A general overall view of Los Angeles Stadium (SoFi Stadium). Mandatory Credit: Kirby Lee-Imagn Images Purchase Licensing Rights

SoFi Stadium hosted eight World Cup matches, including the U.S. opener and two Iran games

Venue will host February's Super Bowl plus opening ‚Äãceremony and swimming at the 2028 Los Angeles Olympics

Benedict said halftime surges by soccer fans tested concourses, ‚Äåconcessions and restrooms

INGLEWOOD, California, July 12 (Reuters) - After a month of World Cup crowds, a temporary grass pitch and heightened security around politically sensitive matches, SoFi Stadium is turning towards its next mega-events with greater confidence, a senior venue executive said.

The $5 billion Inglewood stadium hosted eight ‚ÄãWorld Cup games, including the United States' opening game and two involving Iran, without major incident.

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

It was ‚Äãan experience that Otto Benedict, senior vice president of facility and campus operations at SoFi Stadium ‚ÅÝand Hollywood Park, said validated years of planning.

"From a public safety perspective, I think that went very, very well," ‚ÄãBenedict told Reuters in an interview.

"The number of matches, the size of our matches, having two matches with Iran ... all ‚Äãof our law enforcement partners, our federal assets and even our local assets worked collaboratively to create a very safe environment."

The run served as a high-profile test for a venue that will host February's Super Bowl, as well as swimming and the opening ceremony for the 2028 ‚ÄãLos Angeles Olympics.

Benedict said the stadium's systems for ticketing, crowd movement, food and beverage service and entering and exiting the ‚Äãarena held up well under repeated World Cup conditions, when many fans were visiting the venue for the first time.

"At no point ‚Äåas we ‚ÅÝwalked around did we see areas where we thought, 'Hey, we're actually too tight here, there's not enough space here,'" he said. "We saw everything flowing very well."

Benedict said soccer crowds created a different operational pattern than those at NFL games, with more fans leaving their seats at halftime and putting pressure on concourses, concessions and restrooms at the same time.

The stadium ‚Äãhandled those surges, he added, ‚Äãgiving staff useful information for ‚ÅÝfuture events.

The venue also drew praise for the temporary natural grass surface installed for the tournament.

Benedict said the pitch exceeded expectations and was improving in vitality as the event went ‚Äãon, though he added that keeping grass permanently was not realistic for a stadium that ‚Äãhosts two NFL ‚ÅÝteams, concerts and other events.

"It just wouldn't be a feasible operation," he said, citing the stadium's depth below ground, canopy structure and year-round event schedule.

While Benedict said SoFi definitely should have hosted a semi-final or final, he noted that the World Cup ‚ÅÝnevertheless proved ‚Äãthe stadium was ready for another Super Bowl, after also hosting the ‚ÄãNFL championship game in 2022, and LA28.

"It gives us the ability to say to anybody coming in, and even for our own operations, that what ‚Äãwe're doing is the right consistency," he said. "Let's keep moving these items forward."

(This story has been corrected to clarify that the LA28 closing ceremony will not be held at SoFi Stadium in paragraph 6)

item.title: Lindsey Graham, US Republican senator and Trump ally, dies at 71 from heart ailment
item.description:
By Steve Holland, Akanksha Khushi, Thomas Derpinghaus and Tim Reid

July 12, 20262:22 AM EDTUpdated 1 hour ago
item.content:encoded:
By Steve Holland, Akanksha Khushi, Thomas Derpinghaus and Tim Reid

July 12, 20262:22 AM EDTUpdated 1 hour ago

Graham dies from heart ailment caused by hardening of the arteries

Trump calls Graham a hard-working patriot

Graham went from vocal Trump critic to loyal supporter

Senator was a prominent supporter of Israel and Ukraine

WASHINGTON, July 12 (Reuters) - Republican U.S. Senator Lindsey Graham, a one-time vocal critic of Donald Trump who went on to become one of the president's staunchest allies, died late ‚Äãon Saturday from a heart ailment caused by hardening of the arteries, his office said on Sunday. He was 71.

In an interview with Reuters, Trump said he spoke to Graham "minutes" before the ‚ÄåSouth Carolina Republican was taken ill and ruled out any foul play.

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

"There are these rumors. But, no, I believe he had a very short-term illness. He had a heart attack," Trump said. "He was a friend of mine, a great friend of mine, and it's very devastating losing him."

A preliminary finding from the medical examiner of the District of Columbia said the cause of death was an "aortic dissection," Graham's office said in an email. An aortic dissection is a tear in the main artery that carries blood from the heart. The finding showed it was due to ‚Äãarteriosclerotic cardiovascular disease.

Trump has ordered U.S. flags to be lowered to half-mast in Graham's honor until the night of Saturday, July 18.

The contest to succeed Graham in reliably Republican South Carolina will not impact the broader ‚Äãfight for control of the Senate between Republicans and Democrats in the November midterm elections.

But his death robs Trump of a dependable Senate vote as the president seeks to ‚ÅÝpush his agenda in Congress. With the ongoing absence of another Republican lawmaker, Senator Mitch McConnell of Kentucky, it effectively narrows the party's Senate majority to a minimal 51 votes until a replacement can be sworn in.

Senate Republicans were already down one vote because of ‚Äãthe absence of McConnell, who remains hospitalized recovering from what he described as injuries from a fall at home, as well as mild pneumonia.

Graham died less than two days before the Senate is scheduled to return on Monday from its July ‚Äã4 break for a compressed work period in which Republicans hope to advance key legislation on defense and national security matters and to confirm Trump nominees including the president's former lawyer, Todd Blanche, as U.S. attorney general.

Under South Carolina law, the state's Republican Governor Henry McMaster can immediately appoint a temporary replacement to fill the seat for the remainder of Graham's term, which ends in early January.

Republicans hoping to serve in Graham's place for the next full six-year Senate term will compete in a special party primary election on August 11, with a ‚Äãrunoff set for August 25 if no candidate wins a majority, according to state law. The winner will face Democrat Annie Andrews in the November general election.

STAUNCH ADVOCATE FOR UKRAINE, ISRAEL

Graham, a defense hawk, was a prominent supporter of Israel ‚Äãand Ukraine and an opponent of Iran.

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said he was "deeply saddened" by the news, calling Graham "a true defender of freedom and the values that make our world safer."

On Friday, Graham met Zelenskiy in Kyiv, and the Ukrainian leader said the two discussed ‚ÄåUkraine's air defense ‚ÅÝneeds and a Russia sanctions bill.

Item 1 of 4 U.S. Senator Lindsey Graham speaks during the roundtable with U.S. Border Patrol and members of the Senate Judiciary Committee at a Border Patrol station in McAllen, Texas, U.S. July 12, 2019. REUTERS/Veronica G. Cardenas/File Photo

[1/4]U.S. Senator Lindsey Graham speaks during the roundtable with U.S. Border Patrol and members of the Senate Judiciary Committee at a Border Patrol station in McAllen, Texas, U.S. July 12, 2019. REUTERS/Veronica G. Cardenas/File Photo Purchase Licensing Rights

U.S. Representative Michael McCaul, a Texas Republican and former chairman of the House Foreign Affairs Committee, said Graham's death could strengthen momentum to pass a bill that would increase U.S. sanctions on Russia ‚Äî a Graham-championed initiative that won the White House's endorsement last week.

"The best way we can honor Lindsey is to pass his bill," McCaul told Reuters.

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said in a statement that Israel had lost one of its greatest supporters. "I have lost a beloved friend," Netanyahu added.

Netanyahu expects to attend Graham's funeral, a senior Israeli official said.

During the 2016 presidential campaign, in which Graham was among many Republicans who lost the nomination to Trump, he posted on social media: "If we ‚Äãnominate Trump, we will get destroyed ... and we will deserve ‚Äãit."

Graham told CNN in 2015 that Trump was "a race-baiting, ‚ÅÝxenophobic, religious bigot."

Later, after becoming a loyal supporter and frequent golf partner, Graham still publicly disagreed with Trump's decision upon returning to office last year to pardon about 1,500 of the president's supporters who attacked the U.S. Capitol on January 6, 2021, saying it could lead to more violence.

"They did not always agree, but ... they figured out how to have a ‚Äãfriendship," Republican Senator Tim Scott, Graham's colleague from South Carolina, told "Meet the Press."

Graham rose to prominence in Washington in the late 1990s when he was chosen as a manager for ‚Äãthe House impeachment case against President ‚ÅÝBill Clinton. The House impeached Clinton, but the Senate acquitted him and he remained in office.

More recently, when Graham chaired the Senate's Judiciary Committee, he helped drive Trump's judicial overhaul, presiding over the confirmation of more than 200 federal judges, a conservative legacy likely to last generations.

In 2018, Graham passionately defended Brett Kavanaugh, who was nominated by Trump for a seat on the U.S. Supreme Court, amid sexual assault allegations against the nominee. Kavanaugh was narrowly confirmed to the high court.

item.title: World Cup reflects growing US appetite for soccer, 'Ted Lasso' creators say
item.description:
July 11, 20267:31 PM EDTUpdated 19 hours ago

Item 1 of 2 Soccer Football - FIFA World Cup 2026 - Quarter Final - Argentina v Switzerland - Kansas City Stadium, Kansas City, Missouri, U.S. - July 11, 2026 Actors Jason Sudeikis and Brendan Hunt pitchside before the match REUTERS/Lee Smith
item.content:encoded:
July 11, 20267:31 PM EDTUpdated 19 hours ago

Item 1 of 2 Soccer Football - FIFA World Cup 2026 - Quarter Final - Argentina v Switzerland - Kansas City Stadium, Kansas City, Missouri, U.S. - July 11, 2026 Actors Jason Sudeikis and Brendan Hunt pitchside before the match REUTERS/Lee Smith

[1/2]Soccer Football - FIFA World Cup 2026 - Quarter Final - Argentina v Switzerland - Kansas City Stadium, Kansas City, Missouri, U.S. - July 11, 2026 Actors Jason Sudeikis and Brendan Hunt pitchside before the match REUTERS/Lee Smith Purchase Licensing Rights

KANSAS CITY, Missouri, July 11 (Reuters) - The idea that soccer is not popular in the U.S. is "a little bit overstated" and the World Cup has helped prove it, "Ted Lasso" co-creator and ‚Äãstar Brendan Hunt said, acknowledging the Emmy-winning comedy may have helped draw new fans ‚Äåto the sport.

Speaking to reporters in Kansas City ahead of the quarter-final between Argentina and Switzerland on Saturday, Hunt said he had heard anecdotal evidence that viewers who once ignored soccer became interested through the Apple ‚ÄãTV series.

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

The comedy, which follows a U.S. coach unexpectedly hired to manage an English ‚Äãsoccer club, became a breakout hit during its three-season run from 2020 to ‚ÅÝ2023, winning 13 Emmy Awards, including two for best comedy series.

Asked whether he credited the ‚Äãshow with helping boost soccer's popularity in the U.S., co-creator and star Jason Sudeikis joked: "I do ‚Äãwithin my own home."

"It's made it more popular," he added, before deferring to Hunt, who plays Coach Beard.

Hunt joked that the show's creators had "tricked" some reluctant viewers into embracing the sport. Still, he argued soccer's growth in ‚Äãthe world's No.1 economy predates the series and is often underestimated.

The U.S. is co-hosting the World ‚ÄãCup with Mexico and Canada, more than three decades after staging the 1994 tournament.

While that event helped spark ‚Äåa ‚ÅÝrise in the sport's popularity, with Major League Soccer launching two years later, soccer remains far from dominant there despite the likes of Pele, David Beckham and now Lionel Messi having played in the country.

Hunt said U.S. fans were far more prepared for this World Cup than in ‚Äã1994, when awareness of the ‚Äãtournament was lower.

"We're ‚ÅÝnot as far behind in terms of the sport's popularity as is made out to be, and I think this World Cup has shown ‚Äãthat," he said.

"The stuff about soccer being not popular here is a ‚Äãlittle bit ‚ÅÝoverstated ... It's getting there.

"Now, because every World Cup gets bandwagon fans in every country, whether some of them stick around a little bit more remains to be seen," he added.

The future of "Ted Lasso" ‚ÅÝhad long ‚Äãbeen uncertain after its creators said they originally envisioned a ‚Äãthree-season run, but a fourth season was announced in March 2025.

It premieres on Apple TV on August 5, with Ted ‚Äãreturning to Richmond to coach a second-division women's team.

item.title: From Algerian fervour to Oranje march, World Cup transforms Kansas City
item.description:
July 11, 20267:10 PM EDTUpdated July 11, 2026

Item 1 of 2 Soccer Football - FIFA World Cup 2026 - Group J - Argentina v Algeria - Kansas City Stadium, Kansas City, Missouri, U.S. - June 16, 2026 Algeria fans inside the stadium before the match REUTERS/Claudia Greco TPX IMAGES OF THE DAY
item.content:encoded:
July 11, 20267:10 PM EDTUpdated July 11, 2026

Item 1 of 2 Soccer Football - FIFA World Cup 2026 - Group J - Argentina v Algeria - Kansas City Stadium, Kansas City, Missouri, U.S. - June 16, 2026 Algeria fans inside the stadium before the match REUTERS/Claudia Greco TPX IMAGES OF THE DAY

[1/2]Soccer Football - FIFA World Cup 2026 - Group J - Argentina v Algeria - Kansas City Stadium, Kansas City, Missouri, U.S. - June 16, 2026 Algeria fans inside the stadium before the match REUTERS/Claudia Greco TPX IMAGES OF THE DAY Purchase Licensing Rights

Kansas City hosts six matches including Saturday's quarter-final

More than 310,000 fans from over 150 nations attended Kansas City's FIFA Fan Fest, KC 2026 CEO Kramer says

Netherlands' Oranje Fanwalk draws 36,000 supporters, the largest Dutch turnout in the U.S.

KANSAS CITY, Missouri, July 11 (Reuters) - As Kansas City prepared to bring the curtain down ‚Äãon its World Cup on Saturday, local organisers were already reflecting on a month that put the Midwestern city on the global ‚Äåstage.

Kansas City has been host to six games including a round-of-16 match and Saturday's quarter-final between Argentina and Switzerland at Arrowhead Stadium, the home of the National Football League's Kansas City Chiefs.

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

But the tournament's legacy will be measured as much by the supporters who adopted the city as their temporary home.

The city welcomed large followings from several nations, including Algeria, whose fans spilled ‚Äãacross neighbouring Lawrence, Kansas, where the North African team made their base camp.

A sea of Netherlands orange marched through downtown streets before turning parts ‚Äãof the city centre into a Dutch carnival.

Supporters from Argentina, Switzerland, Colombia and others packed fan zones, bars and public ‚ÅÝsquares over the past month, transforming the self-styled "Soccer Capital of America" into a meeting place for the world and giving residents a rare chance to experience ‚Äãthe World Cup's unique blend of sport, culture and community on their own doorstep.

"The last month has been very special for Kansas City to have a chance to ‚Äãbe on the international stage," Clark Hunt, honorary co-chair of the KC 2026 board and chairman and CEO of the Chiefs, told reporters.

"Getting to see fans from all around the world coming to Kansas City to cheer on their teams ... has been amazing."

Hunt said he hoped the city would have the chance to welcome the tournament back one day.

"If we have the ‚Äãopportunity to bid, we'll do it," he said.

For KC 2026 Chief Executive Pam Kramer, the tournament's legacy will extend beyond football.

"We've proven we can handle the ‚Äãbiggest sporting competition in the world," she said, adding that the World Cup had showcased Kansas City as a destination for business, investment and new residents.

Asked what ‚ÅÝshe would remember most, Kramer recalled a chance conversation with an elderly gentleman that captured the spirit of Kansas City's World Cup.

"(He) said, 'You know, I didn't know much about soccer before and, I have to tell you, I thought it was maybe going to be overblown, it seemed like much to do about nothing,'" Kramer said.

"But what seems to have happened is that people put aside their differences and cheered for the teams for a couple of weeks, and I thought that was ‚Äãso insightful to what has happened ‚Äãhere ... that will stick with me," ‚ÅÝKramer added.

Author and longtime soccer commentator Nate Bukaty said he will remember the spirit of connection.

"Everything I loved about what the game of soccer does has been here for the past month," he said of Kansas City. "And that's the part, I ‚Äãthink, more than the soccer itself, that I will cherish. I'm already kind of nostalgic about it and a ‚Äãlittle sad that it's ‚ÅÝalmost over."

For years, Kansas City wondered how the world would view the city. After a month of packed stadiums, Bukaty believes it got its emphatic answer.

"The question I heard over and over was, 'What's the world going to think of us when they get here?'" he said. "My response was, 'Is the world ready for Kansas City?' ... I feel like everybody's ‚ÅÝbeen pleasantly ‚Äãsurprised with this place. And I think we've acquitted ourselves very well."

Argentina, England, the Netherlands and ‚ÄãAlgeria held their base camps in Kansas City.

Kramer said more than 310,000 fans, from more than 150 nations, had attended the city's FIFA Fan Fest Kansas City, with thousands more expected for Saturday's ‚Äãquarter-final.

The 36,000 supporters who participated in the Netherlands' "Oranje Fanwalk" were the largest Dutch turnout in the U.S.

Writing by Lori Ewing, reporting by Iain Axon; Editing by Ken Ferris

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles.

item.title: US Postal Service raises stamp price to 82 cents
item.description:
July 11, 20263:33 PM EDTUpdated July 11, 2026

A person enters a United States Postal Service (USPS) Post Office in Manhattan, New York City, U.S., May 9, 2022. REUTERS/Andrew Kelly Purchase Licensing Rights
item.content:encoded:
July 11, 20263:33 PM EDTUpdated July 11, 2026

A person enters a United States Postal Service (USPS) Post Office in Manhattan, New York City, U.S., May 9, 2022. REUTERS/Andrew Kelly Purchase Licensing Rights

WASHINGTON, July 11 (Reuters) - The cash-strapped U.S. Postal Service will raise ‚Äãthe price of stamps for ‚Äåmailing a first-class letter to 82 cents from 78 cents, effective Sunday.

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

USPS, which has warned ‚Äãit could run out of cash ‚ÅÝearly next year, announced in ‚ÄãApril it would raise mailing costs ‚Äãby 4.8%.

U.S. Postmaster General David Steiner told Congress last month the agency, which has ‚Äãhad net losses of about $120 ‚Äãbillion since 2007, has a broken business model ‚Äåand ‚ÅÝneeds help from lawmakers to turn around its operations.

The volume of first-class mail, its most profitable product, ‚Äãhas dropped ‚Äãto ‚ÅÝ1960s levels as communication has largely gone digital. Yet ‚Äãthe agency must maintain costly ‚Äãnationwide ‚ÅÝdelivery operations.

Steiner thinks Americans would be willing to pay 90 or ‚ÅÝ95 ‚Äãcents per letter, when ‚Äãmuch of the world pays $2 or more.

item.title: Trump revives praise for his health, cognitive test in post about May physical
item.description:
July 11, 202612:42 PM EDTUpdated July 11, 2026

A picture of U.S. President Donald Trump is displayed at Walter Reed National Military Medical Center on the day of his visit, in Bethesda, Maryland, U.S., May 26, 2026. REUTERS/Kylie Cooper/File Photo Purchase Licensing Rights
item.content:encoded:
July 11, 202612:42 PM EDTUpdated July 11, 2026

A picture of U.S. President Donald Trump is displayed at Walter Reed National Military Medical Center on the day of his visit, in Bethesda, Maryland, U.S., May 26, 2026. REUTERS/Kylie Cooper/File Photo Purchase Licensing Rights

July 11 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump said on ‚ÄãSaturday that he had "just finished" a physical at Walter ‚ÄåReed National Military Medical Center, referring to the medical examination he underwent in late May after which the White House said he was ‚Äãin excellent health.

"I just finished a perfect physical at ‚ÄãWalter Reed, I do it every six months, and ‚ÅÝI requested another Cognitive Test, the only President to ‚Äãdo so, three times, and I aced them all ‚Äî Got every ‚Äãquestion right," Trump, who turned 80 in June, posted on social media.

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

The White House said Trump was referring to his May physical.

He included the ‚Äãremarks in a social media post attacking New York Times ‚Äãreporter Maggie Haberman and her colleague Jonathan Swan, who recently published a ‚Äåbook ‚ÅÝabout Trump's return to power that has drawn widespread attention.

Haberman and Swan's "Regime Change: Inside the Imperial Presidency of Donald Trump" described concerns among some White House aides about Trump's age, ‚Äãstamina and physical ‚Äãcondition.

At ‚ÅÝ80, Trump is the oldest person to serve as U.S. president. Questions about the health and ‚Äãmental fitness of aging leaders have become a ‚Äãrecurring ‚ÅÝissue in Washington after concerns over former President Joe Biden's cognitive abilities ultimately led him to end his 2024 reelection campaign.

Trump ‚ÅÝhas frequently ‚Äãtouted his performance on cognitive tests, ‚Äãsaying he has taken them multiple times and received perfect scores.

item.title: Trump administration subpoenas New York Times journalists over Air Force One story, newspaper says
item.description:
July 11, 202611:49 AM EDTUpdated July 11, 2026

U.S. President Donald Trump deboards the new Air Force One, a plane gifted by Qatar, upon arrival at Ellsworth Air Force Base in Belle Fourche-Cheyenne Valleys, South Dakota, U.S., July 3, 2026. REUTERS/Kylie Cooper Purchase Licensing Rights
item.content:encoded:
July 11, 202611:49 AM EDTUpdated July 11, 2026

U.S. President Donald Trump deboards the new Air Force One, a plane gifted by Qatar, upon arrival at Ellsworth Air Force Base in Belle Fourche-Cheyenne Valleys, South Dakota, U.S., July 3, 2026. REUTERS/Kylie Cooper Purchase Licensing Rights

The United States Department Of Justice

WASHINGTON, July 11 (Reuters) - The New York Times said on Saturday that the U.S. Department of Justice had ordered several of its journalists to testify before a federal grand ‚Äãjury after they reported on security concerns involving President Donald Trump's new Qatari-donated Air Force One.

The ‚Äåmedia organization said subpoenas were issued on Friday, asking that the journalists appear before a grand jury on Wednesday to testify "in regard to an alleged violation of federal criminal law."

The Reuters Inside Track newsletter is your essential guide during the World Cup. Sign up here.

The subpoenas were issued by Jay Clayton, the U.S. ‚Äãattorney in Manhattan, and in some cases delivered to reporters' homes by federal agents, the ‚Äãnews outlet reported. It described the move as "an extraordinary escalation in President Trump's efforts ‚ÅÝto threaten and intimidate independent news organizations."

In a statement to Reuters, a Department of Justice spokesperson ‚Äãdid not confirm or deny the subpoenas but said the administration was not targeting reporters but was ‚Äãconcerned about people leaking classified information. The White House referred all questions to the Justice Department.

Journalism advocacy groups criticized the subpoenas as a threat to press freedom and constitutional rights.

The National Press Club urged the Justice Department to "immediately" withdraw ‚Äãthe subpoenas.

"When federal agents arrive at the homes of journalists with subpoenas, it is not ordinary law ‚Äãenforcement," the club said in a statement. "It is an extraordinary assault on the freedom of the press that strikes ‚Äåat ‚ÅÝthe heart of the First Amendment."

Clayton was recently nominated by Trump to lead the Office of the Director of National Intelligence. The Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press called on the U.S. Senate Intelligence Committee to hold Clayton accountable when he appears for his confirmation hearing next Wednesday.

"When the public‚Äôs right ‚Äãto know is crushed, ‚Äãas the Trump Administration ‚ÅÝis trying to do with its subpoenas against The New York Times, all of us suffer irreparable harm, as does the freedom upon which this ‚Äãnation is built," Stephen J. Adler, chairman of the Reporters Committee, said in ‚Äãa statement.

Trump ‚ÅÝsaid on Wednesday that he would use an older Air Force One "for old time's sake" to fly from Ankara to the Royal Air Force base at Mildenhall in Britain while the new plane stopped at the same ‚ÅÝbase ‚Äãso U.S. service members stationed there could tour the aircraft.

Video ‚Äãlate on Wednesday showed Trump boarding the new Air Force One gifted by Qatar at the British base as it prepared to fly ‚Äãto the U.S.

item.title: US Democrat Ro Khanna detained by Israeli settlers during West Bank visit
item.description:
July 11, 20267:14 AM EDTUpdated July 11, 2026

Khanna makes three-day visit to Palestinian West Bank
item.content:encoded:
July 11, 20267:14 AM EDTUpdated July 11, 2026

Khanna makes three-day visit to Palestinian West Bank

He says Israeli settlers detained his tour group

Israeli military says officers dispersed the settlers

Khanna says Palestinians, Gaza a 'moral test' for Democrats

TURMUS AYYA, West Bank, July 11 (Reuters) - U.S. Democratic lawmaker Ro Khanna said he was detained by Israeli settlers armed with U.S.-made rifles during a West Bank visit this week that ‚Äãhe cast as an unfiltered look at the human toll of Israeli occupation as he weighs a 2028 presidential run.

Speaking with Reuters on Thursday in ‚Äåa Palestinian village, Khanna said his group's van was surrounded by settlers wielding M4 rifles a day earlier while touring a part of the southern West Bank where residents face frequent settler attacks.

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

"We were at a village that Israeli settlers had destroyed, they had destroyed the school, they had destroyed that village, and we were just looking at it," said Khanna, a progressive lawmaker from California in the U.S. House of ‚ÄãRepresentatives.

"And these hoodlums come in with machine guns ‚Äì M4, an American-made machine gun ‚Äì and they detain us. They block off the road. And then they call the IDF and ‚Äãthe IDF is on their side, not on the side of the Americans," Khanna said, referring to the Israeli military.

An aide to ‚ÅÝKhanna who was in the group, Cameron Kasky, said they were held for more than an hour and made appeals to the U.S. Embassy in Jerusalem for help. A group ‚Äãof officers who appeared to be police eventually intervened, leading to their release, Kasky said.

The Israeli military said troops and police officers intervened after receiving a report of settlers blocking ‚Äãvehicles near Khirbet Zanuta, a small Palestinian hamlet whose residents were forcibly displaced by violent settler raids following the 2023 Hamas attacks on Israel.

"Upon their arrival, the troops dispersed the Israeli civilians and allowed the vehicles to continue on their way," the military said.

Israel's police did not immediately respond to a request for comment, nor did the U.S. Embassy in Jerusalem.

DEMOCRATS DIVIDED OVER ISRAELI CONDUCT

Khanna is the second Democrat considering a ‚ÄãWhite House bid to visit the region this week. In Tel Aviv on Wednesday, Rahm Emanuel, who was chief of staff to former President Barack Obama, said Israeli policies toward ‚ÄãPalestinians were eroding support for the U.S.-Israeli alliance.

Item 1 of 5 U.S. Representative Ro Khanna (D-CA) speaks with a Palestinian resident of Turmus Ayya, near Ramallah, during a visit in the Israeli-occupied West Bank, July 9, 2026. REUTERS/Ammar Awad

[1/5]U.S. Representative Ro Khanna (D-CA) speaks with a Palestinian resident of Turmus Ayya, near Ramallah, during a visit in the Israeli-occupied West Bank, July 9, 2026. REUTERS/Ammar Awad Purchase Licensing Rights

Asked if he was running for president, Khanna said: "I'm strongly considering it and I'm more resolved to consider it after this trip."

Israel's conduct toward Palestinians has emerged ‚Äåas a ‚ÅÝflashpoint in Democratic politics ahead of November's U.S. midterm elections, contributing to primary defeats for some incumbent lawmakers targeted by left-wing challengers who accused them of supporting Israel's right-wing government.

Israel's favorability rating among Democrats fell from 59% in 2018 to 22% in May, according to Reuters/Ipsos polling.

While Israel has long enjoyed strong bipartisan U.S. support, an increasing number of Democrats in Congress are now pressing to cut off military aid, which amounts to $3.8 billion per year and includes funding for light weaponry like M4 rifles and missile interceptors that Israel used in ‚Äãthe Iran war.

Overlooking a valley dotted with settler ‚Äãoutposts on the outskirts of Turmus Ayya, ‚ÅÝa village home to thousands of Palestinian American dual nationals, Khanna said he believed his party's establishment was "clueless about how much of a moral test Palestine, Gaza and Israel have become."

He said he chose to do a visit exclusively to the West Bank, with programming led ‚Äãby Palestinians, to give him an unfiltered view of territory Israel captured in the 1967 Middle East war.

"If you're unwilling to ‚Äãspeak up for Palestinian ‚ÅÝhuman rights, if you're unwilling to speak up against the genocide in Gaza, the apartheid in the West Bank, then you are morally compromised," Khanna said.

Israel rejects allegations it carried out a genocide in Gaza or that it institutes an apartheid regime in the West Bank, which has a population of about 3 million Palestinians and around 500,000 Jewish settlers.

Most countries and the United Nations ‚ÅÝregard Israeli ‚Äãsettlements in the West Bank as illegal under international law, citing the Fourth Geneva Convention's prohibition on transferring ‚Äãa civilian population into occupied territory.

Israel rejects that position, saying the West Bank is disputed territory where there has been a Jewish presence for thousands of years. Palestinians view the West Bank, together with Gaza and East ‚ÄãJerusalem, as part of a Palestinian state.

Support remains strong among Republicans, though some elements of Trump's coalition have also called for cutting off aid.

item.title: Flash flooding traps hundreds of people in rural Missouri
item.description:
July 10, 202610:21 PM EDTUpdated July 11, 2026

Thunderstorms dump up to a foot of rain in hours
item.content:encoded:
July 10, 202610:21 PM EDTUpdated July 11, 2026

Thunderstorms dump up to a foot of rain in hours

Heavy downpours turn Black River into raging torrent

National Guard evacuates summer camp with helicopters

July 10 (Reuters) - Flash flooding unleashed by torrential downpours from a wave of thunderstorms struck the Ozark Mountains in ‚Äãrural southeastern Missouri on Friday, trapping hundreds of people in high water along the rain-swollen Black River, ‚Äåofficials said.

National Guard teams in Black Hawk helicopters were flying some 200 stranded people to higher ground on Friday afternoon from Camp Taum Sauk in Lesterville, Missouri, about 100 miles (160 km) south of St. Louis, according to State Highway Patrol Sergeant Eddie Young.

Stay up to date on the key companies, data, and decisions in the ESG world with the Reuters Sustainable Finance newsletter. Sign up here.

About half of the evacuees from the ‚Äãsummer youth camp were children, and the rest were counselors and other staff, Young said.

Separately, rescue teams in boats ‚Äãrescued three people who were stranded elsewhere along the river in Reynolds County late in the day, ‚ÅÝhe said. As of Friday evening, no fatalities had been reported.

Earlier in the day, the Reynolds County Sheriff's Office reported ‚Äãthat emergency teams had rescued more than 90 people from floodwaters that inundated homes, campsites and vehicles.

Joann Franklin was one of them, ‚Äãtelling St. Louis television station KMOV-TV, a CBS affiliate, that she, her husband and their dog and cat were rescued from the roof of their house.

"This is the highest that the water's ever been, and I've lived here since 1979, so (almost) 50 years," she said.

Conditions became so precarious that ‚Äãtwo rescue boats carrying safety workers capsized in the turbulent waters, but crew members were all safely recovered downstream by fellow ‚Äãemergency personnel, the sheriff's office said.

Another 20 to 30 people listed as missing in Reynolds County were later rescued or turned up safe, ‚Äåincluding a ‚ÅÝgroup reported to have been swept off the roof of a building as it collapsed in floodwaters at the Bearcat Getaway Campground, Young said.

One other person remained missing in adjacent Crawford County, he said.

Numerous roads were washed out through the region, making it difficult for emergency crews to reach many riverfront campgrounds to ensure that nobody else was trapped, but search teams planned to return ‚Äãto those spots as floodwaters ‚Äãreceded, Young said.

The Black River ‚ÅÝand nearby streams were transformed into raging torrents by a series of thunderstorms that dumped 6 to 12 inches (150 to 300 mm) of rain on the region overnight and early Friday morning, ‚Äãaccording to the sheriff's office. Additional showers were expected on Friday night in the rain-saturated ‚Äãregion.

Several counties in ‚ÅÝthe mountainous, hilly Lead Belt of southeastern Missouri, in the eastern Ozarks, were the hardest hit, officials said. But stormy weather and the potential for further havoc stretched across a much larger region.

Flood watches were posted in parts of eight states encompassing more than 21 ‚ÅÝmillion people ‚Äãfrom Missouri east through southern Illinois and Indiana and into Kentucky and Tennessee, ‚Äãand north into West Virginia, Ohio and western Pennsylvania, according to the National Weather Service.

Missouri Governor Mike Kehoe declared a state of emergency in flood-stricken regions ‚Äãto ease coordination between state and local agencies and hasten disaster assistance.

item.title: US acting spy chief announces new round of staff reductions
item.description:
July 10, 20269:32 PM EDTUpdated July 10, 2026

Acting U.S. Director of National Intelligence Bill Pulte attends a rally kicking off the Great American State Fair, marking the 250th anniversary of U.S. independence, on the National Mall in Washington, D.C., U.S., June 24, 2026. REUTERS/Jonathan Ernst Purchase Licensing Rights
item.content:encoded:
July 10, 20269:32 PM EDTUpdated July 10, 2026

Acting U.S. Director of National Intelligence Bill Pulte attends a rally kicking off the Great American State Fair, marking the 250th anniversary of U.S. independence, on the National Mall in Washington, D.C., U.S., June 24, 2026. REUTERS/Jonathan Ernst Purchase Licensing Rights

WASHINGTON, July 10 (Reuters) - Acting U.S. Director of ‚ÄãNational Intelligence Bill Pulte announced a third round of staff ‚Äåreductions on Friday after being urged by President Donald Trump in recent weeks to shrink the agency's ranks.

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

"U.S. National Intelligence is operating more efficiently and ‚Äãeffectively than ever before, and today, we started a third ‚Äãround of reducing redundant, or non-critical, personnel," Pulte said in ‚ÅÝa statement on X.

It did not provide the number of roles ‚Äãor personnel that were being terminated.

Since taking office early last year, the ‚ÄãTrump administration has attempted to remake U.S. agencies and institutions by installing Trump loyalists and cracking down on dissent.

The Trump administration has gutted agencies in the name of tackling ‚Äãwasteful spending and fraud but political experts have warned that the ‚Äãeffort has led to elimination of vital roles across government bodies.

Pulte, an ally of ‚ÄåTrump, ‚ÅÝhas no prior national security experience, causing Trump to face a political backlash after the Republican leader's loyalist was picked to fill the role temporarily.

Pulte's appointment had raised concerns even among some Republicans that he ‚Äãwould "weaponize" intelligence against ‚ÄãTrump's perceived political ‚ÅÝfoes.

Reuters reported last month that staff at the office of the top U.S. spy were told by managers to ‚Äãexpect extensive cuts in the coming months following comments ‚Äãby Trump ‚ÅÝthat he wants the interim director to shrink the agency's ranks.

The nomination hearing for Jay Clayton, Trump's nominee to serve as the next U.S. ‚ÅÝdirector of ‚Äãnational intelligence to lead the nation's 18 ‚Äãintelligence agencies, is scheduled for July 15.

Former Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard left office ‚Äãon June 19.

item.title: Trump officials sought ways to sidestep election agency before firings, sources say
item.description:
July 10, 20268:41 PM EDTUpdated July 11, 2026

A man votes on a voting machine at the Beverly Hills City Hall voting center in Beverly Hills, California, U.S., October 30, 2025. REUTERS/Daniel Cole/File Photo Purchase Licensing Rights
item.content:encoded:
July 10, 20268:41 PM EDTUpdated July 11, 2026

A man votes on a voting machine at the Beverly Hills City Hall voting center in Beverly Hills, California, U.S., October 30, 2025. REUTERS/Daniel Cole/File Photo Purchase Licensing Rights

White House and intelligence officials had met to discuss election commission's future

Officials proposed declaring national emergency and creating federal task force on voting

Trump's plans for election commission unclear

Democrats accuse administration of power grab over elections ahead of midterms

WASHINGTON, July 10 (Reuters) - The White House spent months looking for ways to bypass a federal election agency and use emergency powers to force changes to ‚Äãvoting machines, before President Donald Trump ousted its leaders on Thursday, four people familiar with the matter said.

Some officials were frustrated with what they saw as the Election Assistance ‚ÄåCommission's slowness in updating guidelines for states on voting machines, the sources said, while some also wanted it to add a proof-of-citizenship requirement to its national mail voter registration form and address other election-related priorities of the administration.

Jumpstart your morning with the latest legal news delivered straight to your inbox from The Daily Docket newsletter. Sign up here.

Democratic lawmakers criticized the firings as an attempt to increase its control over U.S. elections, which are the purview of the states, and as undermining election integrity ahead of November's midterm elections, when control of Congress will be at stake.

Trump fired the bipartisan ‚Äãfederal agency's two Democratic commissioners and allowed its lone Republican commissioner to resign, Reuters reported on Thursday. The agency's fourth commissioner departed in April.

It was not immediately clear why Trump decided to ‚Äãforce the commissioners out at this time or if they will be replaced. The agency remains operational, but without a quorum it cannot take up ‚ÅÝany new business, such as implementing changes to voting procedures or the national mail voter registration form.

"The administration from the start has been working across all agencies and local partners to safeguard elections ‚Äãfrom fraud and abuse, and investing in a strong infrastructure to sustain that mission especially in the midterm elections," the White House said in a Friday statement when asked about the discussions on sidestepping the ‚Äãcommission.

Trump and his allies have pressed Congress to adopt nationwide voting changes and argued that some voting systems require upgrades, as Trump continues to falsely claim that the 2020 election was stolen from him.

In a Thursday statement confirming the firings, the White House cited a Supreme Court decision in June that granted the president more power to fire members of independent agencies.

"(The president) reserves the right to remove individuals that may not be totally aligned with the important task of securing America‚Äôs ‚Äãelections," the statement said.

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer, a New York Democrat, called the dismissals a ‚Äúbrazen attempt to seize control of our elections before a single vote is cast‚Äù in the midterms.

‚ÄúHe is ‚Äãgutting the independent agency that certifies voting systems and helps election officials run secure elections," Schumer said.

OFFICIALS DISCUSSED PROPOSAL FOR NATIONAL EMERGENCY

As early as last fall, White House officials reviewed a recommendation from the Office of the Director ‚Äåof National ‚ÅÝIntelligence to declare a national emergency and create a federal task force that could compel states to address vulnerabilities in voting systems, without going through the elections commission, according to the four sources.

The ODNI did not respond to a request for comment.

The agency at that time was finalizing its probe of voting machines it had seized from Puerto Rico.

ODNI officials concluded that there were flaws in the Puerto Rico machines that they believed could exist elsewhere, two of the sources said. Election experts have said the U.S. territory, which does not vote in presidential elections, lags the states in implementing the latest voting system guidelines.

The report ‚Äãwas never published and the recommendation was never acted ‚Äãupon, but complaints about the elections commission continued, ‚ÅÝthe two sources said.

During the same period, the two sources said, officials from the Department of Homeland Security, ODNI and the White House met with the commission's leaders to discuss their concerns, including flaws that they believed could have contributed to abnormalities in 2020 ‚Äî claims that have been widely debunked.

The elections ‚Äãcommission is responsible for setting guidelines for states on voting machine systems. Some Trump officials have argued internally that there are states operating with ‚Äãoutdated software, three of the ‚ÅÝsources familiar with the conversations said, and felt the agency was moving too slowly to push for updates.

Election administration experts said the commission often moves slowly in its work because voting systems are complex, the technology is evolving, and policy changes involve extensive public feedback.

‚ÄúThe voting system guidelines haven‚Äôt been updated too frequently because the process takes a long time,‚Äù Matt Weil, vice president of governance at the Bipartisan Policy Center and a ‚ÅÝformer commission staffer, ‚Äãsaid in an interview. ‚ÄúSo yes, there is slowness, but that is not a bug, that‚Äôs a feature of the system.‚Äù

The ‚Äãremaining commission staff can still test and certify equipment, as well as publish research and reports and distribute federal grant money, according to two people familiar with the process.

Congress approved $45 million for the commission in fiscal year 2026 for grants to states ‚Äãto improve election systems. Since 2018, the commission has distributed more than $1.4 billion for election administration, according to the Congressional Research Service.

item.title: Charlie Kirk's widow views video she requested of husband's alleged killer
item.description:
July 10, 20266:54 PM EDTUpdated July 10, 2026

Item 1 of 2 Tyler Robinson listens during a preliminary hearing for Robinson, the Utah man accused of fatally shooting Charlie Kirk, at the 4th District Court in Provo, Utah, U.S., July 10, 2026. Tess Crowley/Pool via REUTERS
item.content:encoded:
July 10, 20266:54 PM EDTUpdated July 10, 2026

Item 1 of 2 Tyler Robinson listens during a preliminary hearing for Robinson, the Utah man accused of fatally shooting Charlie Kirk, at the 4th District Court in Provo, Utah, U.S., July 10, 2026. Tess Crowley/Pool via REUTERS

[1/2]Tyler Robinson listens during a preliminary hearing for Robinson, the Utah man accused of fatally shooting Charlie Kirk, at the 4th District Court in Provo, Utah, U.S., July 10, 2026. Tess Crowley/Pool via REUTERS Purchase Licensing Rights

Judge will not decide whether suspect stands trial until after September 1 arguments

Suspect faces seven criminal charges, including aggravated murder

Prosecutors are seeking death penalty

Kirk's widow as victim representative has pushed for transparency

July 10 (Reuters) - Erika Kirk was allowed on Friday to watch surveillance video, as she ‚Äãhad requested, that prosecutors say shows the Utah man accused of killing her husband, right-wing activist Charlie Kirk.

While the proceedings of the five-day hearing, on ‚Äåwhether Tyler Robinson should stand trial in the case, have mostly been livestreamed in accordance with Erika Kirk's call for transparency, the enhanced video compilation of recordings by campus surveillance cameras was shown only to those present in the courtroom.

Jumpstart your morning with the latest legal news delivered straight to your inbox from The Daily Docket newsletter. Sign up here.

Reuters was not in the courtroom and was unable to view the video, which prosecutors said shows Robinson in several different areas of Utah Valley University on September 10, the day Charlie Kirk was ‚Äãkilled on the campus.

The Associated Press reported that Erika Kirk watched intently as the video showed a person running across the roof of the campus building from ‚Äãwhich investigators say Kirk was shot. When the figure was shown dropping to a crawl near the roof's edge, AP reported that Erika Kirk ‚ÅÝturned and embraced Kirk's crying mother and the two looked away until the video was almost over.

As Charlie Kirk's legal representative under state law, Erika Kirk has called for all ‚Äãevidence presented at the hearing to be made public via a livestream, or otherwise shown to herself and others present in the courtroom.

Jeffrey Neiman, her lawyer, argued in a court filing on ‚ÄãWednesday that, without transparency, "speculation and conspiracy theories related to the tragic assassination of Mr. Kirk will continue to proliferate."

District Court Judge Tony Graf, who viewed the surveillance video earlier in the week but had not allowed it to be shown publicly in court, has weighed the request for transparency against concerns that allowing the public to see certain evidence at this preliminary stage would make it difficult to find impartial jurors ‚Äãif a trial is held.

Graf had called for parts of a pivotal interview with Robinson's roommate to be redacted, and some prosecution evidence has only been displayed in the courtroom, not publicly ‚Äãlivestreamed.

The judge will rule later this year on whether Robinson should stand trial, after hearing oral arguments scheduled for September 1.

The 31-year-old Charlie Kirk, a prominent ally of President Donald Trump, was ‚Äåkilled in ‚ÅÝfront of thousands as he debated students at Utah Valley University. It was one of the highest-profile incidents among a series of attacks on politicians and prominent figures that have intensified concern over U.S. political violence. Trump's son, Donald Trump Jr., attended the first day of this week's hearing alongside Kirk's wife.

Erika Kirk, 37, a podcaster, businesswoman and former Miss Arizona USA, took over leadership of Turning Point USA, the conservative group her husband co-founded, following his death.

Utah, like most states, grants a deceased person's family the right to be informed of court proceedings and ‚Äãto be heard at sentencing hearings. Erika Kirk, ‚Äãthrough her lawyer, has argued in court ‚ÅÝthat Utah‚Äôs crime victim law allows her, as a victim representative, to see all evidence presented in court.

In a statement released on Friday after court proceedings adjourned, the Kirk family said: "As difficult as these last few days have been, it brings our family comfort to know that ‚Äãthe world has witnessed the overwhelming evidence of what occurred to Charlie that day."

The defense has argued that prosecutors will represent certain ‚Äãevidence, such as the ‚ÅÝinterview with Robinson‚Äôs roommate, as "confessions" that he killed Kirk.

Any potential jurors seeing such evidence could be biased, violating Robinson‚Äôs constitutional rights to a free and impartial trial, his lawyer Richard Novak said.

On Thursday, prosecutors displayed a handwritten note that the court had barred from public view. In the note, left under his computer keyboard, Robinson wrote: "I had the opportunity to take out Charlie Kirk, and I'm going ‚ÅÝto take ‚Äãit."

As punishment for the airing of the note, on Friday Graf imposed a one-day moratorium on livestreaming any images ‚Äãentered as evidence. That was not related to his earlier decision not to allow livestreaming of the compilation video Erika Kirk asked to see.

Robinson, who was studying to be an electrician at the time of the shooting, faces ‚Äãseven criminal charges including aggravated murder. Prosecutors are seeking the death penalty. He has yet to enter a plea.

item.title: Trump administration ties states' anti-terrorism grants to election security
item.description: The U.S. Department of Homeland Security said on Friday that states must adopt "common-sense election security measures" to receive certain funds from the Federal Emergency Management Agency.
item.content:encoded:
The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

Reporting by Jasper ‚ÄãWard in Washington; Additional reporting by Costas Pitas; Editing by Daphne Psaledakis, Sergio Non and Tom Hogue

Our Standards: The Thomson Reuters Trust Principles., opens new tab

Thomson Reuters

Jasper Ward is a breaking news reporter in Washington. She primarily covers national affairs and U.S. politics. Jasper was previously based in The Bahamas where she covered the collapse of FTX and the subsequent arrest of its founder Sam Bankman-Fried. She was a part of the Reuters team that won the Gerald Loeb Award for breaking news for its FTX coverage.

item.title: California cannot seek damages from 23andMe successor over 2023 data breach
item.description: A U.S. bankruptcy judge on Friday said California cannot seek damages ‚Äãfrom the company formerly known as 23andMe arising from a 2023 data ‚Äåbreach that exposed genetic and other personal information of an estimated 6.9 million customers.
item.content:encoded:
The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

Reporting by Jonathan Stempel in New York; editing by David Gaffen

item.title: Maine Senate candidate Graham Platner formally withdraws from race
item.description:
July 10, 20264:59 PM EDTUpdated July 10, 2026

Democratic U.S. Senate candidate Graham Platner speaks at a campaign town hall meeting in Ogunquit, Maine, U.S., October 22, 2025. REUTERS/Brian Snyder/File Photo Purchase Licensing Rights
item.content:encoded:
July 10, 20264:59 PM EDTUpdated July 10, 2026

Democratic U.S. Senate candidate Graham Platner speaks at a campaign town hall meeting in Ogunquit, Maine, U.S., October 22, 2025. REUTERS/Brian Snyder/File Photo Purchase Licensing Rights

July 10 (Reuters) - Maine Democratic Senate nominee Graham Platner has officially withdrawn from ‚Äãthe state's U.S. Senate race, the Maine secretary of ‚Äåstate's office said in a statement on Friday, meaning his name will not appear on the November 3 ballot and his political party has until ‚ÄãJuly 27 to name a replacement.

Platner's formal withdrawal came one ‚Äãmonth after he earned the Democratic Party nomination to ‚ÅÝchallenge Republican Senator Susan Collins. Platner easily won the June ‚Äã9 Democratic primary and became crucial to Democrats' drive to win ‚Äãcontrol of the Senate from Republicans in the November 3 election.

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

His fall came following a series of accusations this week, including one of rape, which he has ‚Äãdenied.

"I write to formally withdraw my candidacy for United States ‚ÄãSenate," Platner wrote in a letter to the Maine secretary of state's office.

‚ÄåThe ‚ÅÝletter outlined major themes of his now-doomed campaign, including his drive for national healthcare. He concluded with an expletive aimed at the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agency that is at the center ‚Äãof President Donald Trump's ‚Äãmigrant deportation program ‚ÅÝand a call for a "Free Palestine."

There already are six Democrats who have formally announced campaigns to ‚Äãreplace Platner, in a procedure yet to be detailed ‚Äãby ‚ÅÝthe Maine Democratic Party.

An outline of the process has been announced that would have party delegates gathering at a nominating convention, occurring ‚ÅÝbefore ‚Äãthe July 27 deadline for submitting the ‚Äãnew nominee's name that will appear on November ballots.

item.title: US pain at the pump worsens after more US-Iran fighting lifts oil prices
item.description:
July 10, 20264:38 PM EDTUpdated July 10, 2026

Gasoline prices are displayed on a gas pump in Del Mar, California, U.S., March 3, 2026. REUTERS/Mike Blake/File Photo Purchase Licensing Rights
item.content:encoded:
July 10, 20264:38 PM EDTUpdated July 10, 2026

Gasoline prices are displayed on a gas pump in Del Mar, California, U.S., March 3, 2026. REUTERS/Mike Blake/File Photo Purchase Licensing Rights

AAA data showed average US gasoline prices rose 6 cents this week to $3.88 a gallon Friday

Refinery outages in Russia and the United States are squeezing fuel supplies

EIA data showed US petroleum products exports hit a weekly record 8.7 million bpd

NEW YORK, July 10 (Reuters) - U.S. drivers have seen gasoline prices climb again after weeks ‚Äãof steady declines, as renewed fighting between the U.S. and Iran lifted crudeoilprices to their biggest weekly rise in eight weeks.

Disruptions in the global refining ‚Äåsystem and robust U.S. fuel exports further tightened supplies, and average pump prices rose 6 cents this week to $3.88 a gallon on Friday, AAA data showed. It was the biggest weekly increase since mid-May.

The Reuters Power Up newsletter provides everything you need to know about the global energy industry. Sign up here.

Renewed fighting between the U.S. and Iran over the Strait of Hormuz sent energy prices back up sharply this week. With the U.S. summer driving season in full swing, stubbornly high gasoline prices have emerged as ‚Äãa political flashpoint for President Donald Trump, whose Republican Party is campaigning to hold thin majorities in the U.S. Congress in November midterm elections.

Trump recently ‚Äãaccused oil companies of price gouging.

"Gasoline prices have rallied alongside the massive move higher in crude oil after several tankers ‚ÅÝin the Strait of Hormuz were attacked," Alex Hodes, director of energy market strategy at brokerage StoneX, said.

Global oil futures benchmark Brent was on track for a ‚Äãweekly gain of roughly 5.5% after attacks on several tankers transiting the Strait of Hormuz. This was followed by tit-for-tat strikes between the U.S. and Iran and Washington's decision to revoke a general license ‚Äãpermitting the sale of Iranian oil.

Oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz remain well below pre-conflict levels, stoking fears that even minor disruptions could ripple through global fuel markets. The key waterway carried about 20% of daily global oil and gas supplies before the start of the war on February 28.

Trump has pressed gasoline retailers to cut prices more aggressively. The administration has urged ‚Äãthe U.S. Justice Department to investigate possible gasoline price gouging and recently introduced a new price-cutting initiative offering discounted gasoline at some locations in Pennsylvania and New ‚ÄãJersey.

Crude supply concerns are only part of the story, Hodes added, pointing to unplanned refinery outages in both Russia and the U.S. that have squeezed fuel supplies.

Russia's refining sector has ‚Äåbeen disrupted as repeated ‚ÅÝattacks reduced fuel production and worsened shortages. Moscow has curbed diesel exports and boosted gasoline imports, tightening global fuel supplies and lifting prices.

Russian output of gasoline, diesel, jet fuel and fuel oil has been decimated with many months of downtime ahead, said Tom Kloza, chief energy adviser at Gulf Oil.

In the U.S., refinery outages have further strained supplies, including disruptions at Marathon Petroleum's (MPC.N)

146,000-barrel-per-day refinery in Detroit, Michigan, and Delta's (DAL.N)

190,000-barrel-per-day refinery in Trainer, Pennsylvania.

U.S. gasoline inventories fell by 1.9 million barrels last week to 212.1 million barrels, the Energy ‚ÄãInformation Administration said on Wednesday, leaving stockpiles ‚Äãnearly 10 million barrels below the ‚ÅÝfive-year average.

Gasoline stocks are running below seasonal norms across all U.S. regions, but the shortfall is especially pronounced on the Gulf Coast, said Denton Cinquegrana, chief oil analyst at Dow Jones Energy.

Inventories in the U.S. Gulf Coast, which produces the vast ‚Äãmajority of the nation's supply of refined products, fell to 76.4 million barrels last week, below the five-year average ‚Äãof 82.3 million barrels.

Moreover, ‚ÅÝthe loss of Middle Eastern and Russian barrels from the global market has allowed refiners in the U.S. to reap stronger margins as the swing suppliers of fuel.

U.S. petroleum products exports hit a weekly record of 8.7 million bpd in the week to July 3, EIA data showed.

"The U.S. Gulf of Mexico may see consistent gasoline exports of ‚ÅÝ1-million b/d ‚Äãand there are bets among Houston traders as to whether 2-million b/d will be achieved for ‚Äãdistillate departures," Kloza wrote to clients on Thursday.

The U.S. summer driving season from June through early September typically boosts gasoline consumption, while production of more expensive summer-blend fuel raises refining costs, boosting pump prices.

"It seems prices will ‚Äãmostly drift up and down here in the short-term," Cinquegrana said.

item.title: Family demands answers in death of young Black man in Mississippi
item.description:
July 10, 20264:37 PM EDTUpdated July 11, 2026

July 10 (Reuters) - A mother on Friday pleaded for anyone to come forward with information about what happened to her son, a young Black man whose body was found on an island off the coast ‚Äãof Mississippi after he traveled there over the Fourth of July weekend with three white friends.
item.content:encoded:
July 10, 20264:37 PM EDTUpdated July 11, 2026

July 10 (Reuters) - A mother on Friday pleaded for anyone to come forward with information about what happened to her son, a young Black man whose body was found on an island off the coast ‚Äãof Mississippi after he traveled there over the Fourth of July weekend with three white friends.

"We just want ‚Äåto know what happened and why our baby didn't come home," Christine Wonsley, choking back tears, said at a press conference. The death of her son, Nolan Wells, has become a social media flashpoint, fueled by questions about race in America.

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

The body of Wells, 18, was found on July 6 on the northwestern tip ‚Äãof Horn Island, a long, thin barrier island with a beach along the Gulf of Mexico. He had visited the ‚Äãisland and went missing on the Fourth of July holiday with a group of friends he had known ‚ÅÝfrom high school in nearby Ocean Springs, Mississippi. A lawyer for his family on Friday sought help in answering questions about issues ‚Äãincluding why his phone was not found with his body but with one of the friends.

Across social media, users have parsed timelines and ‚Äãcirculated videos that authorities say remain unverified. For some Black Americans, the case has sparked painful discussions about navigating predominantly white spaces and racism, while civil rights leaders and others have renewed longstanding concerns about disparities in attention to missing persons cases involving Black victims.

As calls have grown for a thorough and ‚Äãtransparent investigation, some people have urged caution against drawing conclusions before authorities release their full findings.

Christine and Elmore Wonsley attend a press conference regarding the death of their son Nolan Xavier Wells who died on an island off the coast of Mississippi, in Manhattan, New York City, U.S., July 10, 2026. REUTERS/David 'Dee' Delgado Purchase Licensing Rights

ABC News has reported that investigators said they ‚Äãsuspect that Wells drowned, but that nothing had been ruled out. The Jackson County Sheriff's Office, the lead investigative agency, told Reuters on Friday that ‚Äåthe investigation ‚ÅÝwas ongoing and active, but provided no other details.

There are many troubling questions about the case, civil rights attorney Ben Crump, who represents the family and is aiding an independent investigation including a private autopsy, said at Friday's press conference in New York with Rev. Al Sharpton and Wells' parents.

The main question, according to Crump, is that the group of three friends who traveled with Wells to the island ‚Äãsaid Wells told them he wanted ‚Äãto stay on the island ‚ÅÝwith a young woman when they left on the afternoon of July 4, but the young woman has said that Wells got on the boat with the boys. Crump also said that videos circulating ‚Äãonline allegedly show Wells in a heated argument with his friends. The videos could not immediately ‚Äãbe verified by Reuters.

Also ‚ÅÝraising suspicions, according to Crump, is that Wells' phone was found not with his body, but in the possession of one of the young men who had accompanied him to Horn Island. Wells' mother tracked it down using Life360, and she said it appears that several social media ‚ÅÝmessages had ‚Äãbeen deleted by someone from the phone.

In a July 7 statement, the Jackson ‚ÄãCounty Sheriff's Office asked for the public's help in providing any eyewitness accounts or video and photo evidence related to anything they might have seen on Horn Island on ‚ÄãJuly 4 involving the Wells case.

(This story has been corrected to fix the mother's last name to 'Wonsley,' in paragraph 2)

item.title: Witnesses to fatal Houston ICE shooting challenge agency's account, lawyer says
item.description:
By Arathy Somasekhar, Evan Garcia and Kristina Cooke

July 10, 20262:22 PM EDTUpdated July 11, 2026
item.content:encoded:
By Arathy Somasekhar, Evan Garcia and Kristina Cooke

July 10, 20262:22 PM EDTUpdated July 11, 2026

Attorney Hugo Baldero-Ybera said witnesses disputed ICE's claim that an officer was in danger

None of the officers involved in Tuesday's shooting were wearing body cameras, DHS said

A DHS official told Reuters that Salgado was not the target of the ICE operation

HOUSTON, July 10 (Reuters) - Three men ‚Äãwho witnessed the killing of Lorenzo Salgado Araujo by a U.S. agent in Houston on Tuesday have challenged the explanation offered by Immigration and Customs ‚ÄåEnforcement, a lawyer for two of the witnesses told reporters in a press conference on Friday.

The three witnesses, including Salgado's brother, are being held at the Montgomery Processing Center in Conroe, Texas. They were riding to work with Salgado in his white van on Tuesday morning when they were pulled over by ICE officers. Their attorney, Hugo Baldero-Ybera, said they have given him a "completely different" account of what happened ‚Äãthat day.

The Reuters Daily Briefing newsletter provides all the news you need to start your day. Sign up here.

He demanded that the men be released immediately to "ensure the integrity" of the investigation into the incident. He also expressed concern that the government would pressure ‚Äãthem to sign paperwork allowing for their deportation.

The shooting sparked protests in Houston's heavily Hispanic East End. More than 1,000 people marched peacefully ‚ÅÝthrough the neighborhood on Wednesday and residents have laid flowers and candles at the scene.

Hours after the incident on Tuesday, ICE said in a statement that Salgado, a Mexican national ‚Äãliving in the U.S. illegally for more than three decades, rammed a law enforcement vehicle with his van and attempted to run over an officer, who then fired on him in ‚Äãself-defense. The agency has not provided evidence to corroborate its version of events.

"At no point was there ever an agent directly in front of the vehicle, nor was an agent ever placed in the line of danger," said Baldero-Ybera, summarizing his clients' accounts. The men also told him that the fatal shots came from the side of the van rather than the front, Baldero-Ybera added.

ICE and the U.S. Department ‚Äãof Homeland Security did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.

Speaking to reporters outside the White House on Friday, Tom Homan, President Donald Trump's Border Czar, said, "If the ‚Äãofficers acted outside of policy or illegally, they'll be held accountable."

Salgado was not the target of the ICE operation that ended in his death, a DHS official told Reuters on Friday.

Hugo Balderas-Ibarra, an immigration attorney representing the three men who were passengers in the van driven by Salgado Araujo, speaks during a press conference regarding the fatal shooting of Mexican motorist Lorenzo Salgado Araujo by an ICE agent, in Houston, Texas, U.S., July 10, 2026. REUTERS/Antranik Tavitian Purchase Licensing Rights

Weeks ‚Äåbefore the ‚ÅÝshooting, while conducting surveillance, immigration agents noticed two white vans at the property of their intended target, the official said. On Tuesday morning, on their way back to that address, they saw a white van "with an individual who resembled the target" and initiated the vehicle stop, the official said.

Salgado's "only crime was he fit the description of another man they were looking for," Baldero-Ybera said.

Family members, local activists and members of the U.S. Congress have demanded an independent investigation into Salgado's killing. The father of three was a construction worker who had lived ‚Äãin Houston for 35 years and was in ‚Äãthe process of obtaining a work ‚ÅÝpermit, relatives said on Wednesday.

The full circumstances of Tuesday's fatal encounter remained unclear.

Surveillance footage first reported by local CBS affiliate KHOU11

shows an unmarked ICE vehicle appearing to cut off Salgado's van in traffic, then shows the van pulling over to the side of the road.

Other ‚Äãverified videos have emerged from the aftermath of the shooting. In one, agents are seen standing over a man clutching his chest. ‚ÄãIn another

, a man can be ‚ÅÝheard crying out in pain. No footage has yet surfaced that captures the moment of the shooting.

The Trump administration moved last year to slow-walk a pilot program to give ICE officers body cameras, urging Congress to cut funding by 75%, Reuters reported in January. DHS said on Thursday that none of the officers involved in the incident were wearing one.

There were also no dashboard ‚ÅÝcameras in the ‚ÄãICE vehicles that captured the shooting, Texas Rep. Sylvia Garcia said at the press conference on Friday. And ‚Äãthere was no clear timeline for the investigation, which ICE said would be led by DHS.

The Harris County District Attorney has appealed to the public to come forward with videos, photos and eyewitness accounts, but stressed that ‚Äãfederal authorities were handling all aspects of the case.
